content:
✅ Approved the contribution.
Comment: Very short, easy and fun in clash.

Hey! Small nitpick, but there are plenty of possible rounding algorithms and your description doesn't specify which one, e.g. in Python:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/decimal.html#rounding-modes

Based on your Rust solution, it seems what you actually mean is "truncate to 1 decimal places" / "only print 1 digit after the decimal point".
https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/fmt/#precision
> For floating-point types, this indicates how many digits after the decimal point should be printed.

I'm not entirely sure it could cause problems because most programming languages usually use either banker's rounding or half-up/half-down, so it works for your test cases and validators, but "rounding" could be interpreted as the ceil type, which would be incorrect. :)

Hi! Cool idea! :) 
Maybe consider adding: 
- Edge cases (E.g. tests evaluating to exactly 5.0 or 30.0)
- A result with multiple decimals (E.g. 7.77 to round to 7.8)

General
* Please study the formatting syntax on this page: https://www.codingame.com/playgrounds/40701/help-center/statement , and format your statement properly as described in the points below.

* The clash currently lacks any key programming elements such as lists, sets, dictionaries, string manipulation, loops, conditions… Please add one or more programming elements so that the clash is more of a programming problem than a maths problem.

Goal
* The units for weight, dose, and drug concentration are not specified. Please clearly state the units.

* The relationship between weight, dose, and concentration is not explained. Please describe how the volume is calculated from these values.

Input
* The description currently placed under "Input" actually describes the output. Please move it to the Output section.

* Please refer to the earlier link for an example of how this section should be written.

Output
* The content under "Output" contains constraints rather than an output description. Please move it to the Constraints section.

Stub generator / Constraints
* Please use the same variable names in the stub generator and the Constraints section.

Constraints
* Please format all [[variables]].

* Please change 150 and 500 to 100, because the inputs never exceed 100.

Thanks.

Thank you for the detailed feedback! I have applied all your suggestions:
- Added units (kg, mg/kg, mg/ml) and the formula in the Goal section
- Moved the output description to the Output section
- Moved the constraints to the Constraints section
- Used consistent variable names (weightKg, doseMg, conc) across the stub generator and Constraints
- Updated the constraints max values to 100

Let me know if anything else needs improvement!

Thank you for the suggestions! 
Let me know if anything else needs improvement!
You're right, "rounded" was ambiguous. I updated the statement to say "truncated to 1 decimal place" with an example (e.g. 10.67 → 10.6) to make it clear. Thanks for the careful review!
Perfect, thanks! :)

There seems to be a misunderstanding. The author's code does not truncate the volume; it rounds it. In fact, only the final pair of cases requires any meaningful rounding.

Based on the author's code:

Test 7
* Volume before rounding: 10.618181818181819
* Output: 10.6 (rounded down)

Validator 7
* Volume before rounding: 11.953125
* Output: 12.0 (rounded up)

Could you please clarify which mode should be used: truncation or rounding? Also, regardless of the intended mode, it would be helpful to include separate pairs of cases demonstrating both scenarios: one where the fractional part is less than 0.5 and one where it is greater.

Thanks!

Thank you for the clarification! The intended behavior is standard rounding (half-up). I have updated the statement accordingly and added two separate test cases:
- One where the fractional part is less than 0.5 (rounds down): e.g. 10.618 → 10.6
- One where the fractional part is greater than 0.5 (rounds up): e.g. 11.953 → 12.0

The Rust solution uses `{:.1}` which applies standard rounding.
Will it be Pair 7 and Pair 8? Because I can't see any changes yet.

Sorry for the confusion! I have now added two separate pairs:
- Pair 7: rounds down (fractional part < 0.5) e.g. 10.618 → 10.6
- Pair 8: rounds up (fractional part > 0.5) e.g. 12.258 → 12.3

Please let me know if anything else needs improvement!

I can only see one “blind spot” left. Players must avoid the trap of whole number division when reducing the dose. For example, with these inputs: 30 7 10, which should give 10.5 and not 9. With this missing test, it's a 10/10 for me :)
Done! I added a test case (30 7 10 → 10.5) and its validator (36 7 12 → 10.5) to catch integer division when halving the dose. Thanks for the feedback!

Thank you. The clash looks better now.

As mentioned before, the clash is more of a programming problem than a maths problem.

Here are a few ideas you might consider (you’re welcome to come up with your own as well) to modify the task:
* add a maximum volume limit
* add a minimum volume limit
* dose is halved if weight is below a certain threshold

Great suggestions! I’ve implemented the min/max volume limits and the weight threshold for halving the dose. This definitely makes the task more of a 'programming problem' rather than just simple math. Thanks for the help—let me know if there's anything else

Goal & Output
* Please change `OVERDOSE` to {{OVERDOSE}}.

* Please change `UNDERDOSE` to {{UNDERDOSE}}.

Goal
* Please change "<" to "is less than".

Goal / Input / Constraints / Stub generator
* You may remove the inputs [[threshold]], [[minVol]] and [[maxVol]] (and the constraints) if they should be the same for every test case. You may state their values in the Goal section instead.

For example, you may write:
If [[weightkg]] is less than {{40}} kg, [[doseMg]] is halved.
(And similarly for the other two variables.)

Cases
* Validator 2: Please revise the case so that the expected output is UNDERDOSE, similar to Test 2. (Each test and its validator should cover the same scenario.)

Thanks.
Please follow up. Thanks.

Sorry for the delay! I have now applied all your suggestions:
- Changed `OVERDOSE` to {{OVERDOSE}} and `UNDERDOSE` to {{UNDERDOSE}}
- Changed "<" to "is less than" in the Goal
- Removed [[threshold]], [[minVol]] and [[maxVol]] from the inputs and stated their fixed values directly in the Goal
- Revised Validator 2 so it covers the UNDERDOSE scenario, consistent with Test 2

Thank you for your patience!

No worries! Just one more thing:

Stub generator
* Please add a write statement, e.g. write answer.

Thanks.
Thanks again , i added the message write answer.
